questions.question_text: Which point lies on the graph of $y = 2x + 1$?

options.why: Worked out $2\times3=6$ but forgot to add the $+1$.
Added the coefficient to $x$ as $3+1$ instead of multiplying by it.
Found the values but wrote the coordinate as $(y,x)$ instead of $(x,y)$.

questions.question_text: Which point lies on the graph of $y = -2x - 1$?

options.why: Ignored the negative signs and computed $2\times1-1=1$.
Dropped the $-2x$ term and kept only the constant $-1$.
Used $2x+1$, reversing the sign of both terms in the equation.

questions.question_text: The line $y = \frac{1}{2}x + 1$ passes through $(0,1)$. Which other point also lies on it?

options.why: Worked out $\frac{1}{2}\times4=2$ but forgot to add the $+1$.
Halved after adding, computing $\frac{1}{2}(4+1)$ instead of $\frac{1}{2}(4)+1$.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=9,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={-1,1,3,5,7,9},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.7:3.9,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,1) (1,3) (2,5) (3,7)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Which equation matches the line shown?

options.why: Read the gradient as $1$; the line actually rises $2$ for each $1$ across.
Misread the $y$-intercept as $-1$; the line crosses the $y$-axis at $+1$.
Counted the rise as $3$ instead of $2$ for each step across.

questions.solution_text:
The line crosses the $y$-axis at $(0,1)$, so $c=1$.
From $(0,1)$ to $(1,3)$ it rises $2$ across $1$, so $m=2$.
$y=2x+1$

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=5,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.7:4.7,samples=2] {-x+4};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,4) (1,3) (2,2) (3,1) (4,0)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Which equation matches the line shown?

options.why: Missed that the line falls from left to right, which needs a negative gradient.
Misread the $y$-intercept as $-4$; the line crosses the $y$-axis at $+4$.
Reversed both the gradient sign and the intercept sign.

questions.solution_text:
The line crosses the $y$-axis at $(0,4)$, so $c=4$.
From $(0,4)$ to $(1,3)$ it falls $1$ across $1$, so $m=-1$.
$y=-x+4$

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6.4cm,height=5.8cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=8,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={-1,1,3,5,7},minor y tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue,<->,domain=-0.7:4.7,samples=2] {x+1};\addplot[only marks,blue,mark=*,mark size=1.6pt] coordinates {(0,1) (1,2) (2,3) (3,4)};\addplot[thick,red,dashed,<->,domain=-0.7:3.5,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[only marks,red,mark=*,mark size=1.6pt] coordinates {(0,1) (1,3) (2,5) (3,7)};\node[red] at (axis cs:1.5,6.6) {$y=2x+1$};\node[blue] at (axis cs:4.1,3.3) {$y=x+1$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The lines $y = x + 1$ and $y = 2x + 1$ are graphed on the same axes. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Both lines pass through $(0,1)$, and $y = 2x + 1$ is the steeper line.
The lines are parallel because they meet the $y$-axis at the same point.
options.why: A shared $y$-intercept does not make lines parallel; parallel lines have equal steepness and never meet.

options.text: The line $y = x + 1$ is steeper because its equation is listed first.
options.why: Steepness is read from the coefficient of $x$, not the order the equations are written.

options.text: The two lines never cross each other.
options.why: The graphs clearly meet at $(0,1)$; only lines with equal gradients never cross.

questions.solution_text:
Both lines cross the $y$-axis at $(0,1)$.
$y=2x+1$ rises $2$ for each $1$ across, while $y=x+1$ rises $1$, so $y=2x+1$ is steeper.
